prompt: Write a warm greeting to a new colleague in five different languages (your choice). After each, add a one-line note about what's culturally implied by the register you chose.
response:
1. English  
“Hey Sarah, so happy you’re here—can’t wait to see what we’ll build together!”  
(The casual first-name use and “build together” signal an egalitarian, team-oriented culture.)

2. Japanese  
“田中さん、ようこそ。一緒に頑張りましょうね。”  
(Tanaka-san, yōkoso. Issho ni ganbarimashō ne.)  
“Welcome, Tanaka-san. Let’s do our best together.”  
(The –san honorific keeps polite distance while ganbarimashō signals group harmony.)

3. Brazilian Portuguese  
“Oi Carlos, seja muito bem-vindo! Já vai ser da família por aqui.”  
“Hi Carlos, a big welcome—you’ll be family here before you know it.”  
(Informal affection and quick “family” inclusion mirror Brazilian warmth.)

4. Arabic (Levantine)  
“أهلاً وسهلاً يا ليلى، نورتِ الدار!”  
(Ahlan wa sahlan yā Laylā, nawwarti d-dār!)  
“Welcome, Laylā—may your light fill the house!”  
(The poetic “light” phrasing shows traditional hospitality and respect.)

5. German  
“Guten Tag, Herr Neumann, herzlich willkommen in unserem Team!”  
“Good day, Mr. Neumann, a heartfelt welcome to our team.”  
(Titles and last names uphold German business formality; herzlich adds warmth without getting too private.)
